title: Linear search vs binary search

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Search (Brute Force)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the array sequentially to find the target value. Use this when the array is unsorted or small, as it works for any order but is inefficient for large datasets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement and Compare Linear Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear search with comparison to binary search

explanation.prose: This full problem requires implementing linear search from scratch and comparing it to binary search. The intuition is that linear search works by sequentially checking each element until the target is found or the array ends, making it universally applicable but inefficient for large datasets. The algorithm: 1) Accept array and target as input. 2) Initialize index variable to -1 (not found). 3) Loop through each element from first to last. 4) Compare current element with target. 5) If match found, store index and break. 6) Return index. This connects to the broader problem by demonstrating the brute-force approach that binary search improves upon.

commonMistakes: forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return -

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sequential Element Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that compares each array element with target

explanation.prose: This sub-routine focuses on the iterative comparison process that forms the heart of linear search. The intuition is that to find an element in an unordered collection, we must examine elements one by one until we either find a match or exhaust all possibilities. The algorithm: 1) Receive array and target as parameters. 2) Set up a loop counter from 0 to array length minus 1. 3) In each iteration, access the current element using the index. 4) Compare the element's value with the target value. 5) If equal, we've found our target and can terminate early. 6) If loop completes without finding match, target doesn't exist in array. This feeds into the full linear search by providing the actual search mechanism—the full problem adds input validation and return value handling.

timeComplexity.note: where n is array length, as we may need to check every element

commonMistakes: using wrong comparison operator (== vs =), not handling array bounds correctly, and forgetting that array indices start at

Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=1 ≠ 5 → continue
Step 5: Loop finished, no match → return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element-by-Element Traversal with Early Exit
ladders.desc: Understand the sequential checking pattern with optimization

explanation.prose: This core logic demonstrates the fundamental pattern of checking elements in sequence while allowing early termination when the target is found. The intuition is that in an unsorted array, we have no information about where the target might be, so we must check positions systematically. However, once we find the target, further checking is unnecessary—this early exit optimization distinguishes linear search from simply scanning the entire array. The algorithm: 1) Start at the first element (index 0). 2) Check if current element equals target. 3) If yes, stop immediately and report success. 4) If no, move to next element. 5) Repeat until either found or end of array reached. Time complexity ranges from O(1) (if target is first element) to O(n) (if target is last or not present). This connects to the comparison loop by adding the crucial optimization of early exit—without this, we'd waste time checking remaining elements after finding the target.
commonMistakes: continuing to check after finding the target (inefficient)
not considering that the target might appear multiple times (we only need first occurrence)

confusing this with complete array traversal algorithms. This pattern is essential for understanding why linear search can sometimes be fast but is unreliable for large datasets.

Pseudocode:
function sequential_check(arr

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5 ≠ 8 → i=1
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3 ≠ 8 → i=2
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=8 == 8 → break loop
Step 4: i=2 < len(arr)=4 → return 2

Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5 ≠ 4 → i=1
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3 ≠ 4 → i=2
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=8 ≠ 4 → i=3
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=2 ≠ 4 → i=4
Step 5: i=4 not < len(arr)=4 → return -1

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements and compare values

explanation.prose: This building block focuses on two fundamental operations: accessing elements in an array using indices, and comparing values for equality. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a numerical index starting from 0. To search, we need to retrieve each element and check if it matches our target. The algorithm: 1) Understand that arr[i] gives the value at position i. 2) Compare this value with target using equality operator (==). 3) Based on comparison result, take appropriate action. Time complexity for a single access and comparison is O(1). This connects to the sequential checking logic by providing the basic operations performed in each iteration of the search loop. Without proper understanding of array indexing (0-based vs 1-based) and value comparison, the search algorithm cannot function correctly.
commonMistakes: confusing array indices (thinking arr[1] is first element)

not understanding that comparison returns a boolean value. These operations form the foundation of not just search algorithms but most array processing tasks.

Pseudocode:
# Access element at index i
value = arr[i]

# Compare with target
if value == target:
    # Do something when equal
else:
    # Do something when not equal

examples.trace:
Step 1: Access arr[1] → value = 20
Step 2: Compare value (20) with target (20) → 20 == 20 is True
Step 3: Since comparison is True, we have a match

Step 1: Access arr[0] → value = 10
Step 2: Compare value (10) with target (25) → 10 == 25 is False
Step 3: Since comparison is False, no match

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through all valid positions in an array

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves creating a loop that visits each position in an array exactly once, in sequential order. The intuition is that arrays have a fixed number of elements, and we can use a counter variable to track our current position, incrementing it until we've visited all positions. The algorithm: 1) Determine array length n. 2) Initialize counter i to 0 (first index). 3) While i is less than n, we have valid positions to visit. 4) Perform operation at position i. 5) Increment i to move to next position. 6) Repeat until i equals n (all positions visited). This connects to array indexing by providing the mechanism to systematically access every element—without this iteration, we could only access individual positions.
commonMistakes: using i <= n instead of i < n (causes out-of-bounds error)
not understanding that array indices are integers. This iteration pattern is one of the most fundamental in programming

appearing in virtually all algorithms that process collections of data.

Pseudocode:
function iterate_array(arr):
    n = length(arr)
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        # Do something with arr[i]
    # All positions visited

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = 3, i = 0 (valid: 0 < 3) → visit index 0
Step 2: i = 1 (valid: 1 < 3) → visit index 1
Step 3: i = 2 (valid: 2 < 3) → visit index 2
Step 4: i = 3 (invalid: 3 < 3 is false) → stop
Step 1: n = 0, i = 0 (invalid: 0 < 0 is false) → stop immediately

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic array structure and position numbering

explanation.prose:
This foundational concept introduces arrays as ordered collections of elements where each element has a numerical position called an index. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values under one name, with each value accessible by its position number. Arrays have: 1) A fixed size (number of elements). 2) Elements of the same type (typically). 3) Indexing starting at 0 (first element at position 0, second at 1, etc.). 4) Contiguous storage in memory. To work with arrays, you need to understand: how to declare/create an array, how to access elements using bracket notation (arr[index]), and that valid indices range from 0 to length-1. Time and space complexity aren't applicable at this basic level—this is about understanding the data structure itself. This connects to array iteration by establishing what we're iterating over—without understanding that arrays have indices and elements, iteration makes no sense. Common beginner mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1, trying to access indices outside the valid range, and confusing arrays with other data structures. This foundation is essential for all array-based algorithms, not just search. Pseudocode:
# Creating an array
arr = [value1, value2, value3, ..] # Accessing elements
first_element = arr[0] # Index 0 = first position
second_element = arr[1] # Index 1 = second position # Array length
n = length(arr) # Number of elements.

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements: position 0 contains 10, position 1 contains 20, position 2 contains 30. Valid indices are 0, 1, 2. arr[3] would be invalid (out of bounds).
Array has 1 element: position 0 contains 5. Valid indices are only 0. arr[1] would be invalid.
